summary: Nadia argues that software foundations, despite their nonprofit status and warm associations, are structurally problematic for open source projects. Foundations create single points of failure that contradict the inherently distributed nature of open source. She proposes separating the services foundations provide (legal, financial, IP management) from governance, using fiscal sponsors for the former while developing new distributed governance models. The ideal model places the project as sovereign, with companies and organizations as participating actors rather than controlling bodies. Entity(0), the project's originator, should actively work to distribute contribution opportunities rather than consolidate control, because successful projects function more like standards that benefit from propagation than organizations that benefit from centralization.
key_points: Foundations enjoy less public accountability than companies because we assume they're 'default-good' while assuming companies are 'default-bad'
Software foundations conflate distinct needs (financial, legal, IP, governance) that could be better handled separately through specialized service providers
The winner-take-all nature of foundations contradicts open source's distributed model - projects have many contributors but typically only one foundation
A healthier governance model places the project as sovereign with Entity(0) actively working to make themselves just one actor among many
Successful open source projects should function like standards, language, or religion - beyond the control of any single organization
money_quotes: There is nothing about a foundation that is inherently good, neutral, or charitable, simply because they have a 501(c) status.
Foundations suffer from a winner-take-all mentality, where for any given project, only one foundation is assumed to exist.

Just as 'the government' is not 'the people', but rather a representative body whose legitimacy derives from sustained popular support, 'the foundation' is not 'the project', and should not be treated as such.
Rather than checking power with more power, the intended approach is to dilute all central nodes of influence over time, using a decentralized model.
When an open source project succeeds, it looks more like a standard than an organization, and standards succeed when they propagate.
Several corporate contributors have told me they 'launder' their contributions to avoid detection.
Much like language, religion, or etiquette, successful open source projects should exist beyond the control of any one company or foundation.
key_insight: Software foundations create structural single points of failure that undermine open source's greatest strength—its distributed, resilient organizational culture—and projects should instead pursue governance models where the originating entity actively works to become just one participant among many.
